Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

Before you hit ‘share,’ pause and check the source: Is it a well‑known outlet or just a random page? Look for author credentials; a reputable journalist will usually list their name and affiliation. If you can’t find any info, that’s a red flag.

Next, use fact‑checking tools that are straightforward even if your education stops at primary school. Websites like Snopes, FactCheck.org, or the Associated Press Fact Check offer short explanations without jargon. I’ve found it helpful to bookmark one of these sites and open it in a new tab before clicking on a link. If the article turns out false, close the tab and move on—don’t let the false narrative linger.

When a post claims something about faith or community practices, cross‑reference it with official statements from your church or reputable Christian think tanks. Many churches now publish social media guidelines for their congregants, which can serve as a quick filter.

Your conscientiousness comes into play when setting up filters on the platforms you use. Turn on ‘safe search’ or ‘content warnings,’ and adjust your news feed preferences to show fewer sensational headlines. I’ve seen that even small tweaks—like unfollowing accounts that routinely spread rumors—can make a big difference in the quality of information I encounter.

Finally, remember that social media is a community. If you spot fake content, consider gently correcting it rather than outright dismissing the poster. A short note like, ‘I read this on X and found it was debunked by Y,’ can be more effective than shouting back. This approach aligns with your high agreeableness and helps maintain relationships while protecting yourself.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Living in Chicago and juggling a part‑time mechanic job while raising two kids means my time on social media is precious but also risky. I’ve seen how quickly a headline can spread—especially when it taps into the fears that are already heavy for someone dealing with depression. The first rule, then, is to slow down. Before you hit ‘share,’ pause and check the source: Is it a well‑known outlet or just a random page? Look for author credentials; a reputable journalist will usually list their name and affiliation. If you can’t find any info, that’s a red flag.

Next, use fact‑checking tools that are straightforward even if your education stops at primary school. Websites like Snopes, FactCheck.org, or the Associated Press Fact Check offer short explanations without jargon. I’ve found it helpful to bookmark one of these sites and open it in a new tab before clicking on a link. If the article turns out false, close the tab and move on—don’t let the false narrative linger.

Because you’re religious and have strong values, consider how misinformation can erode trust in institutions that matter to you. When a post claims something about faith or community practices, cross‑reference it with official statements from your church or reputable Christian think tanks. Many churches now publish social media guidelines for their congregants, which can serve as a quick filter.

Your conscientiousness comes into play when setting up filters on the platforms you use. Turn on ‘safe search’ or ‘content warnings,’ and adjust your news feed preferences to show fewer sensational headlines. I’ve seen that even small tweaks—like unfollowing accounts that routinely spread rumors—can make a big difference in the quality of information I encounter.

Finally, remember that social media is a community. If you spot fake content, consider gently correcting it rather than outright dismissing the poster. A short note like, ‘I read this on X and found it was debunked by Y,’ can be more effective than shouting back. This approach aligns with your high agreeableness and helps maintain relationships while protecting yourself.

In sum: verify sources, use simple fact‑checkers, align content with trusted religious voices, adjust platform settings, and correct politely. These steps keep the information you consume reliable without sacrificing the social connections that matter to you.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Identified persona traits (age 19, mechanic, primary school education, high conscientiousness, low extraversion, high agreeableness, depressed) and tailored advice accordingly: simple source checks, use of fact‑check sites, religious alignment, platform filters, polite corrections. Maintained 250-500 word range.
